plan.contextChunk:
## Introduction

As objects move in the world, or as the camera moves, the projection of
the dynamic scene into the two-dimensional (2D) camera plane produces a
sequence of temporally varying pixel brightness. Before diving into how
to estimate motion from pixels, it is useful to understand the image
formation process. Studying how three-dimensional (3D) motion projects
into the camera will allow us to understand what the difference is
between a moving camera or a moving object and what types of constraints
one might be able to use to estimate motion.

## 3D Motion and Its 2D Projection {#d-motion-and-its-2d-projection}

A 3D point will follow a trajectory $\mathbf{P}(t) = (X(t),Y(t),Z(t))$,
in camera coordinates (@fig-optical_flow_basic_motion_point). As the
point moves, it has an instantaneous 3D velocity of
$\dot{\mathbf{P}} = (\dot{X}(t), \dot{Y}(t), \dot{Z}(t))$. The
projection of this point into the image plane location is
$\mathbf{p}(t) = (x(t),y(t))$ and its projection will move with the 2D
instantaneous velocity $\dot{\mathbf{p}} = (\dot{x}(t), \dot{y}(t))$,
where all the derivatives are done with respect to time, $t$.

![A 3D point, $\mathbf{P}$, moving in the world projects a 2D moving point, $\mathbf{p}$, into the camera plane.](figures/optical_flow/basic_motion_point.png){width="50%" #fig-optical_flow_basic_motion_point}

Using the equations of perspective projection $x=f X/Z$ and $y=f Y/Z$
(assuming that the camera is at the origin of the world-coordinate
system), we can derive the equations of how the instantaneous velocity
in the camera plane relates to the point motion in 3D world coordinates:
$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= f \frac{\dot{X} Z - \dot{Z} X}{Z^2} = \frac{f \dot{X} - \dot{Z} x}{Z}\\
\dot{y} &= f \frac{\dot{Y} Z - \dot{Z} Y}{Z^2} = \frac{f \dot{Y} - \dot{Z} y}{Z} 
\end{aligned}$$ The second expression is obtained by using $x=f \, X/Z$
and $y=f \, Y/Z$, which removes the dependency on the world coordinates
$X$ and $Y$. Note that $f$ is the focal length. In many derivations, the
notation is simplified by setting the focal length $f=1$. Here we will
keep it to make explicit which factors depend on the camera parameters
and which ones do not. We can write the last two equations in matrix
form as:

elements: 3D origin point labeled O
camera/image plane (rectangle) in 3D space
3D trajectory point labeled P
velocity vector at P (red arrow, labeled \dot{\mathbf{P}})
projection of 3D point P onto image plane (labeled \mathbf{p})
velocity vector at image point (red arrow, labeled \dot{\mathbf{p}})
green vector from O to P (\mathbf{OP})
green vector from O to p (\mathbf{Op})
dashed green projection line from P to image plane (shows projection path)
solid black points at O, P, p
labels for O, P, p, \mathbf{P}, \mathbf{p}, \dot{\mathbf{P}}, \dot{\mathbf{p}}

interactions.label: 3D point position
interactions.teaches: Moving this control changes the depth (Z) of the 3D point, showing how its projection onto the image plane and the velocity vector directions are affected.

interactions.label: 3D point velocity angle
interactions.teaches: Rotating this changes the 3D direction of the point's velocity, illustrating how the projected 2D velocity depends on the world velocity orientation.

interactions.label: Focal length f
interactions.teaches: Altering the focal length changes the scale of the projection and its sensitivity to depth.

demo_steps.title: Start with default setup
demo_steps.narration: Let's begin with the 3D point at a moderate depth and a certain velocity in space. You can see how the 3D velocity vector projects to a shorter vector on the image plane.

demo_steps.title: Vary the point's depth
demo_steps.narration: As we move the 3D point closer to or farther from the camera, notice how its projection shifts and the image-plane velocity changes in both direction and length. The same 3D velocity produces a much faster motion in the image when the point is close.

demo_steps.title: Change 3D velocity direction
demo_steps.narration: Now let’s rotate the 3D velocity vector. When most of the velocity points along the depth (Z) direction, its image-plane motion is smaller. When it points across the image plane (X or Y), the projected speed is larger.

demo_steps.title: Alter the focal length
demo_steps.narration: Increasing the focal length makes the projection larger and stretches both the position and velocity in the image plane. This shows why camera parameters matter for motion estimation: the same physical movement produces different apparent speeds at different focal lengths.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Isometric view angled downward to clearly see both the image plane and the 3D space beyond it, zoomed to fit the plane and moving point comfortably inside the frame.
interactionPlan.notes: Use semi-transparent lines for projection rays and dashed lines for edges behind the image plane. Arrows for velocity vectors should be bright red, with dynamic labels updating as controls change. Make labels always face the camera. Adjust opacity or thickness for lines passing behind the plane to enhance depth cues.

attempts.html:
Start with default setup
Let's begin with the 3D point at a moderate depth and a certain velocity in space.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: Generated figure adds many extra labels (e.g., OP, Op, image plane) not present in source.
The arrowhead sizes and placements (for Ṗ and ṗ) are slightly different from the original.
Green projection line appears solid with a more complex construction, but original only has two clear green vectors (solid and dashed).
The interface allows changes to focal length and velocity, which aren't in the static source.
Camera initial viewpoint places the image plane in a less prominent orientation than in the textbook figure.
evaluation.notes: Concept and geometry are overall accurate and interactive, but there are extra/hallucinated labels and the initial viewpoint is not optimal.
action_items: Remove or hide the OP, Op, and extra 'image plane' labels for closer faithfulness to the source.
Adjust initial camera viewpoint to make the plane orientation match the source more closely (planar face angled toward viewer).
Reduce the text label clutter and verify that the core P, p, O, Ṗ, ṗ labels match placement and style (size/weight/offsets) in the reference.
Consider reducing UI controls or providing a 'static' mode matching the source for close-up fidelity.

feedback.rationale: The main issues involve hallucinated labels, visual styling, initial viewpoint, and extra controls—all execution problems rather than conceptual or planning errors.
action_items: Remove or hide the OP, Op, and extra 'image plane' labels for closer faithfulness to the source.
Adjust initial camera viewpoint to make the plane orientation match the source more closely (planar face angled toward viewer).
Reduce the text label clutter and verify that the core P, p, O, Ṗ, ṗ labels match placement and style (size/weight/offsets) in the reference.
Consider reducing UI controls or providing a 'static' mode matching the source for close-up fidelity.
actionItems: Remove or hide the OP, Op, and extra 'image plane' labels for closer faithfulness to the source.
Adjust initial camera viewpoint to make the plane orientation match the source more closely (planar face angled toward viewer).
Reduce the text label clutter and verify that the core P, p, O, Ṗ, ṗ labels match placement and style (size/weight/offsets) in the reference.
Consider reducing UI controls or providing a 'static' mode matching the source for close-up fidelity.

Start with default setup
Let's begin with the 3D point at a moderate depth and a certain velocity in space. You can see how the 3D velocity vector projects to a shorter vector on the image plane.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The green connecting line from p to P is correctly present, but the dashed green projection line is offset and less visually clear than the textbook figure.
All essential points (O, p, P) and their black disks are present and relatively proportional, though their placement could be better aligned for an identical match.
Arrowheads and vector thicknesses are slightly different, but the red velocity arrows are drawn similarly as in the reference.
Labels are generally correctly placed, but font size and boldness do not fully match the thick textbook markup.
The 3D plane drawing and its wireframe are appropriate, but the subtlety of rendered line style and overlap with 3D geometry differs from the stylized 2D of the reference.
evaluation.notes: Very close to the original with accurate elements, pedagogically helpful interactivity, clear concept, minor differences in label/font styling and line visual expressiveness.
action_items: Increase label font-weights and sizes to more closely match the prominent original (especially for p, P, pdot, Pdot).
Fine-tune the placement of points O, p, P so the geometry more exactly matches the source proportions.
Reduce or eliminate the vertical offset of the dashed green projection line to match the straight projection in the original.
Adjust arrowhead sizes and boldness to better replicate the textbook illustration's emphasis.
Ensure the line thicknesses and black disk halos more closely echo the strong graphical contrasts of the 2D figure.

feedback.rationale: All issues are related to visual style, alignment, thickness, and labels rather than conceptual or interaction plan errors.

Ensure the line thicknesses and black disk halos more closely echo the strong graphical contrasts of the 2D figure.

discrepancies: 3D velocity vectors and their projections are present as red arrows but not exactly matching bold/thickness or the hand-drawn style of the original
The arrangement and length of the green lines differ; in the original, the green lines converge perfectly at p, but here they are distinct segments with soft fades and dashed helpers
Extra green dashed and soft lines are present in the generated version, which do not exist in the original
The projection plane is shown as a rectangle with a subtle fill and extra edge effects, while the original is a simple, bold-edged quadrilateral
Labels are well-placed but slightly larger and more separated than in the original, with heavy font weight, making the scene feel less compact
evaluation.notes: Main concept and projection are faithfully represented with strong interactive value but minor visual and primitive discrepancies exist.
action_items: Remove extra green dashed and soft lines that do not exist in the source figure to avoid distraction and hallucination.
Adjust arrow thickness, style, and head size for closer match to the bold/sharp original figures.
Refine the projection plane to remove the fill and soften the edge effects, matching the simple line drawing of the source.
Reduce label font size and tighten placement to better mimic the original's compact labeling.

feedback.rationale: All action items target visual and stylistic corrections rather than conceptual changes, indicating the plan is correct but the visual generation requires refinement.
